title: Rustic Bathroom Vanity
summary: A three-drawer bathroom vanity with plank sides and a face frame, built from 1x6 pine. Drawers ride on euro slides; the top is built to your sink.
description:
This vanity gets its rustic look from plank-built sides — three 1x6s glued edge to edge instead of a plywood panel — which reads warmer and hides the fact that it's an approachable pine build. Underneath it's real cabinetry: a face frame, three drawers on euro slides, and a top you build around your own sink.

Two details carry the build. First, the sides: glue the 1x6s edge to edge and let them set before adding the 1x2 trim, so you're working with one stable panel instead of three loose boards. Second, the drawers only run true if the case is square, so check the diagonals after the face frame goes on and before you commit the drawer slides. The slides here mount to a 1x2 cleat at the front and a back socket — a system that tolerates a plumbing-dodging drawer if you box out around the trap.

The top is deliberately left to you because sinks vary. The source builder framed 1x boards around the basin — 1x4s front and back, 1x12s on the sides — leaving an opening for a drop-in sink. Build the top last, once the sink is in hand.

tools.note: For notching drawers around plumbing if needed.

materials.name: 1x6, 8 ft
materials.note: Plank sides, back, and drawer boxes.

materials.name: 1x2, 8 ft
materials.note: Side trim, face frame, drawer-slide supports, front trim.

materials.name: 1x4, 8 ft
materials.note: Front legs and top drawer box.

materials.name: Finish
materials.note: A moisture-tolerant finish — this is a bathroom.

cutList.note: Three per side, glued edge to edge.

steps.title: Build the plank side panels
steps.body: Lay three 1x6s down on a flat surface with tops and bottoms even, and glue them edge to edge; let them set so they hold as one panel. Attach the 1x2 trim on top, overhanging the ends by 3/4". Build both sides.

steps.title: Add the back supports
steps.body: Drill two pocket holes on each end of the 1x3 back supports and attach with pocket screws, pockets exposed on the back of the cabinet. These tie the two sides together.

steps.title: Build and attach the face frame
steps.body: Build the front face frame on a flat surface with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket screws, then nail and glue it to the front. Take a moment to confirm the cabinet is square by measuring the diagonals — the drawers depend on it.

steps.title: Add the back planks and front trim
steps.body: Nail and glue the back planks to the sides and top/bottom supports — these give the drawer slides something to fasten to while keeping a finished edge (no exposed plywood on the back). Then measure and cut the front trim to fit and nail and glue it in place.

steps.title: Build the drawers
steps.body: For full-size drawers that clear the plumbing, build the boxes with pocket holes and glue-and-nail the plywood bottoms on. If a drawer has to dodge the trap, keep the same basic cuts but move the back forward past the plumbing and box out the corners as needed.

steps.title: Install the slides and drawers
steps.body: Mount the drawer slides to the 1x2 cleat at the front and the back socket at the rear, then hang the drawers and check for smooth travel. Add the bin pulls.

steps.title: Build the top and finish
steps.body:
Build the top from 1x boards pocket-holed together, leaving an opening for your sink — the source used 1x4s front and back with 1x12s on the sides. Build it last, once you have the sink in hand.

Finish the whole vanity with a moisture-tolerant paint or topcoat.

images.alt: A rustic bathroom vanity with plank sides and three drawers with bin pulls
